title: Ocean Acidification Research Partnerships Act
plain_summary: This bill creates a new program that gives money to help seafood companies work with schools and scientists to study ocean acidification. Ocean acidification happens when the ocean becomes more acidic and can hurt sea animals and plants. The government will pay most of the costs for these research projects, up to 85 percent. The program will get 5 million dollars each year from 2026 to 2030 to fund this research.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Science, Space, and Technology.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Ocean Acidification Research Partnerships Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Ocean Acidification Collaborative Research Grants
sections.text: This section adds a new part to an existing law from two thousand nine about ocean acidification research. The new part creates a grant program for research projects that study how the ocean is becoming more acidic. The grants will go to partnerships between the seafood industry and schools or research institutions. The academic community means teachers, researchers, scientists, and people who manage natural resources from colleges and other schools. The seafood industry means people who grow shellfish, catch fish for business or fun, and organizations that represent these groups. The Secretary will give grants to research projects that bring together the seafood industry and academic community to work together. To get a grant, projects must follow the government's research plan for ocean acidification and meet several goals. Projects must help the seafood industry and academic community work together better and understand each other. They must include seafood industry people in the research and create results that help both groups. Projects must use knowledge from both the seafood industry, who know the ocean environment well, and the academic community. They must help academics better understand what the seafood industry wants to learn about. Projects must also help more people understand ocean acidification and must have balanced support from both the seafood industry and academics. The Secretary will give priority to projects that study areas most hurt by ocean acidification, have support from local groups like state governments or tribes, or use seafood industry boats and equipment as research platforms. Within one hundred eighty days after this becomes law, the Secretary must work with a government committee to create guidelines for how the grants will work. These guidelines must be made after talking with state and local decision makers who know about ocean acidification, the seafood industry and other ocean businesses, teachers both in and out of schools, tribes, environmental groups working on ocean acidification, and other community members. Each grant application must include information about who will do the work and their qualifications. Applications must have a plan showing how both industry and academic partners will fully participate and contribute their expertise to designing, doing, and understanding the research. Applications must include a plan for sharing the research results through education programs, presentations to industry and academic groups and community members, scientific publications, and reports to government officials who will use the information. Applications must describe how the project fits with existing government program goals and include any other information the Secretary needs. The Secretary can give grants to partnerships that include other ocean-related industries instead of the seafood industry if the project still meets the law's goals. In these cases, the other industry takes the place of the seafood industry in all requirements. Each group that gets a grant must give regular reports as required by the Secretary. These reports must include all information the Secretary needs to judge how well the project is working. The federal government can pay for no more than eighty five percent of each project's total cost. The remaining fifteen percent must come from non-federal sources, which can include donated work and other non-cash contributions. The Secretary can waive this matching requirement if there is no reasonable way for applicants to meet it and the project's benefits are more important than requiring matching funds. Congress approves spending five million dollars each year from two thousand twenty six through two thousand thirty to carry out this program.
